(Romanian pronunciation: [[diˈnamo bukuˈreʃtʲ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian")) or simply **Dinamo**, is a Romanian professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest") that competes in the [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I"), the top tier of [Romanian football](/wiki/Romanian_football_league_system "Romanian football league system").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Association football club in Bucharest

This article is about SC Dinamo 1948 (commercial entity), the football club which competes in [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I"). For the football section of the multi-sport, publicly owned club, see [CS Dinamo București (football)](/wiki/CS_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti_(football) "CS Dinamo București (football)"). For the football club owned by Nicolae Badea that formerly competed in [Liga III](/wiki/Liga_III "Liga III"), see [ACS FC Dinamo București](/wiki/ACS_FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "ACS FC Dinamo București").

**FC Dinamo București**, commonly known as **Dinamo București** (Romanian pronunciation: [[diˈnamo bukuˈreʃtʲ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian")) or simply **Dinamo**, is a Romanian professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest") that competes in the [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I"), the top tier of [Romanian football](/wiki/Romanian_football_league_system "Romanian football league system").

Founded in 1948 as the team of the [Ministry of Internal Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Internal_Affairs_(Romania) "Ministry of Internal Affairs (Romania)"), following the merger of [Unirea Tricolor](/wiki/Unirea_Tricolor_Bucure%C8%99ti "Unirea Tricolor București") and [Ciocanul](/wiki/Maccabi_Bucure%C8%99ti "Maccabi București"), Dinamo București has spent all but one year of its history in the top tier (Liga I) of the [Romanian league system](/wiki/Romanian_football_league_system "Romanian football league system"). Domestically, it is one of the two most successful clubs in the country, having won 18 Liga I titles, 13 [Cupa României](/wiki/Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "Cupa României"), two [Supercupa României](/wiki/Supercupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "Supercupa României"), and one [Cupa Ligii](/wiki/Cupa_Ligii "Cupa Ligii"). In the [1983–84 season](/wiki/European_Cup_1983-84 "European Cup 1983-84"), Dinamo became the first club in Romania to reach the semi-finals of the [European Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League"), being eliminated by [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") 1–3 on [aggregate](/wiki/Playoff_format#Total_points_series_(aggregate) "Playoff format").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Dinamo București's traditional home colours are white and red, while the current crest is a modified version of the one adopted in the 1998. Its home venue is the namesake [Stadionul Dinamo](/wiki/Stadionul_Dinamo "Stadionul Dinamo"), although significant matches have also been played at [Arena Națională](/wiki/Arena_Na%C8%9Bional%C4%83 "Arena Națională"). The team is temporarily playing its home fixtures at [Stadionul Arcul de Triumf](/wiki/Stadionul_Arcul_de_Triumf "Stadionul Arcul de Triumf"), as its home ground undergoes demolition.
Its bitter rivals are neighbouring [FCSB](/wiki/FCSB "FCSB"), with the match being commonly referred to as "[the Eternal Derby](/wiki/Eternal_derby_(Romania) "Eternal derby (Romania)")," while notable rivalries against [Rapid București](/wiki/FC_Rapid_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Rapid București") and [Universitatea Craiova](/wiki/CS_Universitatea_Craiova "CS Universitatea Craiova") also exist.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [List of FC Dinamo București seasons](/wiki/List_of_FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti_seasons "List of FC Dinamo București seasons")

### 1948–1955: Foundation and early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1948–1955: Foundation and early years")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti_League_Performance.svg/250px-FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti_League_Performance.svg.png)](/wiki/File:FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti_League_Performance.svg)

Historical chart of FC Dinamo București league performance.

On 14 May 1948, "[Unirea Tricolor MAI](/wiki/Unirea_Tricolor_Bucure%C8%99ti "Unirea Tricolor București")" — newly entered, in January 1948, under the umbrella of the Communist regime's [Internal Affairs Ministry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Administration_and_Interior_of_Romania "Ministry of Administration and Interior of Romania") — merged with "[Ciocanul București](/wiki/Maccabi_Bucure%C8%99ti "Maccabi București")" and formed "Dinamo București", the sporting club representing the above-mentioned institution.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The people who contributed at the foundation of the club were the [Minister of Internal Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Internal_Affairs_(Romania) "Ministry of Internal Affairs (Romania)"), [Teohari Georgescu](/wiki/Teohari_Georgescu "Teohari Georgescu") and the Jewish brothers Alexandru and Turi Vogl.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Raica_cu_echipa_in_%2753.jpg/330px-Raica_cu_echipa_in_%2753.jpg)](/wiki/File:Raica_cu_echipa_in_%2753.jpg)

The Dinamo squad in 1953

Until the end of the championship, Dinamo was going to be represented by two teams: "Ciocanul" ("Hammer"), named "Dinamo A" and "Unirea Tricolor MAI", known as "Dinamo B" (this last one relegating, at the end of the football season, into the [Divizia B](/wiki/Divizia_B "Divizia B")). Starting with 1950, Dinamo A was separated from Dinamo B, the latter being transferred first to [Braşov](/wiki/Bra%C5%9Fov "Braşov"), then to [Cluj-Napoca](/wiki/Cluj-Napoca "Cluj-Napoca"), and eventually, in 1958, being moved to [Bacău](/wiki/Bac%C4%83u "Bacău"), where it became [FCM Bacău](/wiki/FCM_Bac%C4%83u "FCM Bacău").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The "Dinamo" name was used for the first time on 1 May 1948. Nevertheless, the real debut of Dinamo was on the [1947–48 Divizia A](/wiki/1947%E2%80%9348_Divizia_A "1947–48 Divizia A") edition (finishing 8th). Some of the team's players were [Ambru](/wiki/Florian_Ambru "Florian Ambru"), [Angelo Niculescu](/wiki/Angelo_Niculescu "Angelo Niculescu"), Teodorescu, [Șiclovan](/wiki/Ion_%C8%98iclovan "Ion Șiclovan"), [Bartha](/wiki/Carol_Bartha "Carol Bartha") and Sârbu.

On 11 July 1948, Dinamo played its first international match against [SK Židenice](/wiki/FC_Zbrojovka_Brno "FC Zbrojovka Brno") from Czechoslovakia, and winning 4–1.[[9]](#cite_note-9) 22 August will remain a reference date for Dinamo's football, representing the debut of Dinamo Bucharest in the first national Division. The pioneers were, among others, [Titus Ozon](/wiki/Titus_Ozon "Titus Ozon"), [Lăzăreanu](/wiki/Gheorghe_L%C4%83z%C4%83reanu "Gheorghe Lăzăreanu"), [Farkaș](/wiki/Iuliu_Farka%C8%99 "Iuliu Farkaș"). The team's first coach was [Coloman Braun-Bogdan](/wiki/Coloman_Braun-Bogdan "Coloman Braun-Bogdan"). A year later [Nicolae Dumitru](/wiki/Nicolae_Dumitru_(footballer) "Nicolae Dumitru (footballer)") and [Gheorghe Băcuț](/wiki/Gheorghe_B%C4%83cu%C8%9B "Gheorghe Băcuț") join the club. At the end of the 1950 Divizia A edition, Angelo Niculescu retires, dedicating himself to the coaching profession.

In 1955, Dinamo won their first championship with Angelo Niculescu as head coach, Dinamo impressed mainly in the offensive, with an attack formed by [Ene I](/wiki/Alexandru_Ene "Alexandru Ene"), [Neagu](/wiki/Valeriu_Neagu "Valeriu Neagu") and [Suru](/wiki/Ion_Suru "Ion Suru"). The defense, with players like Băcuț I (Gheorghe), [Băcuț II](/wiki/Ladislau_B%C4%83cu%C8%9B "Ladislau Băcuț") (Ladislau), [Szökő](/wiki/Iosif_Sz%C3%B6k%C5%91 "Iosif Szökő"), [Călinoiu](/wiki/Valeriu_C%C4%83linoiu "Valeriu Călinoiu"), was the best in the championship – only 19 goals received.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In the fall of 1956, the team makes its debut in the [European Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") (competition created a year before). Dinamo was the first Romanian club to play in the competition. The debut game was played on 26 August 1956, in front of 32,000 spectators. Dinamo defeated [Galatasaray](/wiki/Galatasaray_S.K._(football_team) "Galatasaray S.K. (football team)") with 3–1. In the second leg, Dinamo lost in Istanbul with 1–2, and moved forward.

### 1950–1981: Domestic success and European competition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1950–1981: Domestic success and European competition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Ion_Nunweiller_%281971%29.jpg/250px-Ion_Nunweiller_%281971%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ion_Nunweiller_(1971).jpg)

[Ion Nunweiller](/wiki/Ion_Nunweiller "Ion Nunweiller"), one of the club legends.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Ion_Parcalab.jpg/250px-Ion_Parcalab.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ion_Parcalab.jpg)

[Ion Pârcălab](/wiki/Ion_P%C3%A2rc%C4%83lab "Ion Pârcălab"), a key member of the great Dinamo team of the 1960s

Dinamo won the second championship in 1962, with players like [Datcu](/wiki/Ilie_Datcu "Ilie Datcu"), [I. Nunweiller](/wiki/Ion_Nunweiller "Ion Nunweiller"), [Pârcălab](/wiki/Ion_P%C3%A2rc%C4%83lab "Ion Pârcălab"), [Frățilă](/wiki/Constantin_Fr%C4%83%C8%9Bil%C4%83 "Constantin Frățilă"), [Popa](/wiki/Cornel_Popa_(footballer) "Cornel Popa (footballer)"), [Varga](/wiki/Iosif_Varga_(footballer) "Iosif Varga (footballer)") (head coaches were [Traian Ionescu](/wiki/Traian_Ionescu "Traian Ionescu"), [Constantin Teașcă](/wiki/Constantin_Tea%C8%99c%C4%83 "Constantin Teașcă") and [Nicușor Dumitru](/wiki/Nicolae_Dumitru_(footballer) "Nicolae Dumitru (footballer)")). In 1963, the club won their third title. Midfielder [Vasile Gergely](/wiki/Vasile_Gergely "Vasile Gergely") joined the team that season.

In the [1963–64](/wiki/1963%E2%80%9364_FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti_season "1963–64 FC Dinamo București season") season, the club won the double with new players such as [Octavian Popescu](/wiki/Octavian_Popescu_(footballer,_born_1938) "Octavian Popescu (footballer, born 1938)") and [Emil Petru](/wiki/Emil_Petru "Emil Petru"). In the [Romanian Cup final](/wiki/1964_Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei_final "1964 Cupa României final") they beat rivals Steaua 5–3. Dinamo played in the [1963–64 European Cup](/wiki/1963%E2%80%9364_European_Cup "1963–64 European Cup") against the famous [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid "Real Madrid") (1–3 in Bucharest), a team with [Di Stefano](/wiki/Alfredo_Di_Stefano "Alfredo Di Stefano") and [Gento](/wiki/Francisco_Gento "Francisco Gento"). The game played in Bucharest took place at ["23 August" Stadium](/wiki/Stadionul_Na%C8%9Bional_(1953) "Stadionul Național (1953)"), and established a new record for this arena: 100,000 spectators.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In the fall of 1964, in the European Cup, Dinamo met [Inter Milan](/wiki/Inter_Milan "Inter Milan") with [Facchetti](/wiki/Giacinto_Facchetti "Giacinto Facchetti"), [Mazzola](/wiki/Sandro_Mazzola "Sandro Mazzola"), [Jair](/wiki/Jair_da_Costa "Jair da Costa"), [Corso](/wiki/Mario_Corso "Mario Corso") and [Suárez](/wiki/Luis_Su%C3%A1rez_(footballer,_born_1935) "Luis Suárez (footballer, born 1935)"). Dinamo lost both legs, 0–6 and 0–1.

In 1965, Dinamo won their fourth [consecutive title](/wiki/1964%E2%80%9365_Divizia_A "1964–65 Divizia A"), with players like [Mircea Lucescu](/wiki/Mircea_Lucescu "Mircea Lucescu"), [Radu Nunweiller](/wiki/Radu_Nunweiller "Radu Nunweiller") and [Gheorghe Ene](/wiki/Gheorghe_Ene "Gheorghe Ene"). The [following season](/wiki/1965%E2%80%9366_European_Cup "1965–66 European Cup") of the European Cup, Dinamo met again Inter Milan, and managed to win the home game, 2–1. Inter won at [San Siro](/wiki/San_Siro "San Siro") 2–0 and moved forward.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Florea_Dumitrache.jpg/250px-Florea_Dumitrache.jpg)](/wiki/File:Florea_Dumitrache.jpg)

[Florea Dumitrache](/wiki/Florea_Dumitrache "Florea Dumitrache")

The club won their 6th title in 1971. In the 1971–72 European Cup campaign, Dinamo eliminated [Spartak Trnava](/wiki/Spartak_Trnava "Spartak Trnava"), the team of [Dobias](/wiki/Karol_Dobi%C3%A1%C5%A1 "Karol Dobiáš"), [Kuna](/wiki/Ladislav_Kuna "Ladislav Kuna") and [Adamec](/wiki/Jozef_Adamec "Jozef Adamec"), and losing in the second round to [Ernst Happel](/wiki/Ernst_Happel "Ernst Happel")'s [Feyenoord](/wiki/Feyenoord "Feyenoord") 0–3 and 0–2. The 7th title comes two years later, in 1973, when Dinamo won a game against [CFR Cluj](/wiki/CFR_Cluj "CFR Cluj") at the exact margins to pass Universitatea Craiova in the standings.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In the 1973–74 European Cup, they surpass [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland")'s [Crusaders Belfast](/wiki/Crusaders_F.C. "Crusaders F.C.") (The 11–0 home game against Northern Ireland's team is still the biggest margin of victory in the history of the [European Cup](/wiki/European_Cup "European Cup")), but fail against [Atlético Madrid](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Madrid "Atlético Madrid") (0–2 and 2–2), the team of [Gárate](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Eulogio_G%C3%A1rate "José Eulogio Gárate"), [Irueta](/wiki/Javier_Irureta "Javier Irureta"), [Heredia](/wiki/Ram%C3%B3n_Heredia "Ramón Heredia") and [Ayala](/wiki/Rub%C3%A9n_Ayala "Rubén Ayala"). The next year Dinamo plays in the [1974–75 UEFA Cup](/wiki/1974%E2%80%9375_UEFA_Cup "1974–75 UEFA Cup") and after eliminating Boluspor, they lose to [F.C. Koln](/wiki/1._FC_K%C3%B6ln "1. FC Köln"): 1–1 and 2–3.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Dudu_Georgescu.jpg/250px-Dudu_Georgescu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dudu_Georgescu.jpg)

[Dudu Georgescu](/wiki/Dudu_Georgescu "Dudu Georgescu"), the club's all-time topscorer with 207 goals

*The Red Dogs* won their 8th title in the [1974–75](/wiki/1974%E2%80%9375_Divizia_A "1974–75 Divizia A") season, [Dudu Georgescu](/wiki/Dudu_Georgescu "Dudu Georgescu") received his first '[Golden Boot](/wiki/European_Golden_Boot "European Golden Boot")' award after scoring 33 goals that year. The title winning squad included young and experienced players like [Sătmăreanu](/wiki/Alexandru_S%C4%83tm%C4%83reanu "Alexandru Sătmăreanu"), [Moldovan](/wiki/Alexandru_Moldovan "Alexandru Moldovan"), [Vrînceanu](/wiki/Cristian_Vr%C3%AEnceanu "Cristian Vrînceanu"), [Marin](/wiki/Marin_Ion "Marin Ion") and [Deleanu](/wiki/Augustin_Deleanu "Augustin Deleanu"). In the [1976–77 UEFA Cup](/wiki/1976%E2%80%9377_UEFA_Cup "1976–77 UEFA Cup"), Dinamo played against [AC Milan](/wiki/AC_Milan "AC Milan") with [Fabio Capello](/wiki/Fabio_Capello "Fabio Capello") and [Collovatti](/wiki/Fulvio_Collovati "Fulvio Collovati") on its side: 0–0 and 1–2.

The 1976–77 season brings the 9th title for the club, and the second 'Golden Boot' for Georgescu (47 goals).[[13]](#cite_note-13) In the autumn of 1977 in the [European Cup](/wiki/1977%E2%80%9378_European_Cup "1977–78 European Cup"), Dinamo wins a thrilling game against Atlético Madrid 2–1, but loses at [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid"), 2–0.

In the second round of the [1979–80 UEFA Cup](/wiki/1979%E2%80%9380_UEFA_Cup "1979–80 UEFA Cup") (after eliminating [Alki Larnaca](/wiki/Alki_Larnaca "Alki Larnaca") from [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus")) Dinamo is eliminated by [Eintracht Frankfurt](/wiki/Eintracht_Frankfurt "Eintracht Frankfurt"), team of [Grabowski](/wiki/J%C3%BCrgen_Grabowski "Jürgen Grabowski") and [Hölzenbein](/wiki/Bernd_H%C3%B6lzenbein "Bernd Hölzenbein") (2–0 and 0–3 in prolongation).[[14]](#cite_note-14) The German side ended up winning the trophy.

### 1981–1992: Golden team

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1981–1992: Golden team")]

The [1981–82 UEFA Cup](/wiki/1981%E2%80%9382_UEFA_Cup "1981–82 UEFA Cup") season brings some great wins for Dinamo. The red-whites meet [Levski Sofia](/wiki/PFC_Levski_Sofia "PFC Levski Sofia") of Bulgaria (3–0 and 1–2). In the second round, a terrifying "double": Dinamo-Internazionale (with Bergomi, Bagni, [Prohaska](/wiki/Herbert_Prohaska "Herbert Prohaska"), [Altobelli](/wiki/Alessandro_Altobelli "Alessandro Altobelli"), [Baresi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Baresi "Giuseppe Baresi"), [Oriali](/wiki/Gabriele_Oriali "Gabriele Oriali"), Marini and Beccalossi). At Milan, 1–1 (authors: Pasinato and Custov) and back home in Bucharest, 3–2 for the "dogs", in the extra time.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Dinamo is eliminated by the Swedish team [IFK Göteborg](/wiki/IFK_G%C3%B6teborg "IFK Göteborg"), which later ends up winning the trophy.

The 10th national title will be obtained in 1982, when Dinamo also conquers the Cup after a 3–2 victory against Baia Mare. After a 5-year absence, Dinamo reappears in the [European Cup](/wiki/1982%E2%80%9383_European_Cup "1982–83 European Cup"), meeting (and eliminating) [Vaaleregen Oslo](/wiki/V%C3%A5lerenga_I.F._Fotball "Vålerenga I.F. Fotball"). In the next stage, Dinamo faced the team of [Dukla Prague](/wiki/Dukla_Prague "Dukla Prague") which included players such as [Nehoda](/wiki/Zden%C4%9Bk_Nehoda "Zdeněk Nehoda") and [Vízek](/wiki/Ladislav_V%C3%ADzek "Ladislav Vízek"). They win at home, with 2–0, obtaining the qualification at Prague: 1–2. Dinamo is eliminated by [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa "Aston Villa"), club of [Cowans](/wiki/Gordon_Cowans "Gordon Cowans"), [Withe](/wiki/Peter_Withe "Peter Withe"), [Shaw](/wiki/Gary_Shaw_(footballer,_born_1961) "Gary Shaw (footballer, born 1961)") and [Morley](/wiki/Tony_Morley "Tony Morley"). In 1983, the club win their 11th title.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Cornel_Dinu_2.jpg/250px-Cornel_Dinu_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cornel_Dinu_2.jpg)

[Cornel Dinu](/wiki/Cornel_Dinu "Cornel Dinu"), the player with the most appearances for Dinamo (454)

The 1983–1984 season began with the retirement of [Cornel Dinu](/wiki/Cornel_Dinu "Cornel Dinu"), winner of 6 national titles and 6 national cups, with 454 appearances in the first league and 75 in the national team. Still the same year Dinamo wins their 12th title. The autumn of [1983–84](/wiki/European_Cup_1983%E2%80%9384 "European Cup 1983–84") was going to represent a valuable step into the international arena. The European Cup campaign started with the Finnish team, [Kuusysi Lahti](/wiki/Kuusysi_Lahti "Kuusysi Lahti") (1–0 and 3–0). The second round pushes Dinamo against the current champion, [Hamburger SV](/wiki/Hamburger_SV "Hamburger SV") – team of [Kaltz](/wiki/Manfred_Kaltz "Manfred Kaltz") and [Magath](/wiki/Felix_Magath "Felix Magath"). At Bucharest, [Augustin](/wiki/Ionel_Augustin "Ionel Augustin"), [Mulțescu](/wiki/Gheorghe_Mul%C8%9Bescu "Gheorghe Mulțescu") and [Orac](/wiki/Costel_Orac "Costel Orac") score for 3–0.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The thrilling second leg finishes 3–2 (goals [Țălnar](/wiki/Cornel_%C8%9A%C4%83lnar "Cornel Țălnar") and Mulțescu). In order to accede in the semifinals of the European Cup, Dinamo had to defeat the Soviet side [Dinamo Minsk](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Minsk "FC Dinamo Minsk"), with [Aleinikov](/wiki/Sergei_Aleinikov "Sergei Aleinikov"), [Zygmantovich](/wiki/Andrei_Zygmantovich "Andrei Zygmantovich") and [Hurynovich](/wiki/Ihar_Hurynovich "Ihar Hurynovich"). The first leg was 1–1 ([Rednic](/wiki/Mircea_Rednic "Mircea Rednic") equalizing in the 87th minute), and it was followed by a 1–0 victory at Bucharest (with Augustin scoring). Dinamo was the first Romanian team to reach the [European Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") semi-finals,[[17]](#cite_note-17) where it met [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C."). Dinamo lost 1–0 at [Anfield](/wiki/Anfield "Anfield") in the first leg match where [Graeme Souness](/wiki/Graeme_Souness "Graeme Souness") had punched captain [Lica Movila](/wiki/Lica_Movila "Lica Movila") which had set up a tough match back in Romania.[[18]](#cite_note-18) In the second-leg Dinamo lost 2–1 to Liverpool who went on to win the competition.[[19]](#cite_note-19) In the first round of the next European Cup, in the autumn of 1984, Dinamo meets [Omonia Nicosia](/wiki/Omonia_Nicosia "Omonia Nicosia"): 4–1 and 1–2. Next is the match against [Girondins Bordeaux](/wiki/Girondins_Bordeaux "Girondins Bordeaux") (club of [Tigana](/wiki/Jean_Tigana "Jean Tigana"), [Giresse](/wiki/Alain_Giresse "Alain Giresse"), [Lacombe](/wiki/Bernard_Lacombe "Bernard Lacombe") and [Battiston](/wiki/Patrick_Battiston "Patrick Battiston")), Dinamo being eliminated after 0–1 and 1–1. In 1986, Dinamo won the Cup against Steaua (1–0). After an 18-year break, Dinamo played again in the Cup Winners Cup in the autumn of 1986. The 1986–87 season brought the 'Golden Boot' for [Cămătaru](/wiki/Rodion_C%C4%83m%C4%83taru "Rodion Cămătaru") (44 goals). The 1988 Romanian cup final was a special one. After Steaua players left the field at the score of 1–1 due to a claim of being robbed by the referee, Dinamo was given the trophy, but later the [Romanian F.A.](/wiki/Romanian_Football_Federation "Romanian Football Federation") (bowing to pressure from the [Communist Party](/wiki/Romanian_Communist_Party "Romanian Communist Party")) awarded the match 2–1 to Steaua. After the [revolution of December 1989](/wiki/Romanian_Revolution_of_1989 "Romanian Revolution of 1989"), Steaua propositioned to return the trophy to Dinamo, which refused to take it. In the [1988–89 Cup Winners Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup_1988%E2%80%9389 "UEFA Cup Winners' Cup 1988–89") season, Dinamo again eliminated the Finnish team Lahti, managing to win 3–0. The [Cup Winners Cup 1989–90](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup_1989%E2%80%9390 "UEFA Cup Winners' Cup 1989–90") season brings new success. Dinamo meets Albanian team, [Dinamo Tirana](/wiki/Dinamo_Tirana "Dinamo Tirana") 0–1 and 2–0. Next is the Greek champion, Panathinaikos 2–0 and 6–1. In the quarter-finals they meet [Partizan Belgrade](/wiki/FK_Partizan "FK Partizan") (with [Mijatović](/wiki/Predrag_Mijatovi%C4%87 "Predrag Mijatović") on the field) eliminating them with 2–1 and 2–0. After six years of break, Dinamo plays a new continental semifinal this time against [Anderlecht Bruxelles](/wiki/RSCA "RSCA"), losing twice with 1–0. In the summer of 1990, Dinamo – with [Mircea Lucescu](/wiki/Mircea_Lucescu "Mircea Lucescu") as coach – conquers a new national title, the 13th. Also the team wins the Cup final, against Steaua: 6–4. The new 1990–91 European Cup season begins with the elimination of Irish club [St Patrick's Athletic](/wiki/St_Patrick%27s_Athletic "St Patrick's Athletic") of Dublin (4–0 and 1–1). Dinamo is eliminated in the second round by [FC Porto](/wiki/FC_Porto "FC Porto"). In the [1991–92 UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_1991%E2%80%9392 "UEFA Cup 1991–92") edition, the club faces [Luís Figo](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Figo "Luís Figo")'s [Sporting Clube de Portugal](/wiki/Sporting_Clube_de_Portugal "Sporting Clube de Portugal"), qualifying after a 0–1 loss and a 2–0 victory. The next round Dinamo is eliminated by Italian side [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa_CFC "Genoa CFC"): 1–3 and 2–2. In 1992, the 14th title was added to Dinamo's record. It was a triumphal march, with 34 matches and no defeat.

### 1992–2013: Ups and downs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1992–2013: Ups and downs")]

The years to come saw Dinamo in the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup"). However, without any special results, the team leaves the competition in the first qualification round, in [1993–1994](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_1993%E2%80%9394 "UEFA Cup 1993–94") losing to [Cagliari](/wiki/Cagliari_Calcio "Cagliari Calcio"), in [1994–95](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_1994%E2%80%9395 "UEFA Cup 1994–95") losing to [Trabzonspor](/wiki/Trabzonspor "Trabzonspor"), and in [1996](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_1995%E2%80%9396 "UEFA Cup 1995–96") losing to [Levski Sofia](/wiki/Levski_Sofia "Levski Sofia").
Among the new players that play for Dinamo are: [Catalin Hildan](/wiki/Catalin_Hildan "Catalin Hildan"), [Florentin Petre](/wiki/Florentin_Petre "Florentin Petre"), [Cosmin Contra](/wiki/Cosmin_Contra "Cosmin Contra"). Alongside the goalkeeper [Florin Prunea](/wiki/Florin_Prunea "Florin Prunea") appear [Mihalcea](/wiki/Adrian_Mihalcea "Adrian Mihalcea") and [Kiriță](/wiki/Giani_Kiri%C8%9B%C4%83 "Giani Kiriță"). With this new team, in the 1998–1999 season Dinamo plays arguably the best football in Romania, though the team loses the title to [Rapid București](/wiki/FC_Rapid_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Rapid București"). Dinamo ended the dark era of the mid-1990s this way once they started to fight for the title this year, only having to wait one more year to win it with no contender. Dinamo played next season in the UEFA Cup beating [Benfica](/wiki/S.L._Benfica "S.L. Benfica") 1–0 on [Estádio da Luz](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_da_Luz_(1954) "Estádio da Luz (1954)"), then losing a suspected game on [Lia Manoliu](/wiki/Lia_Manoliu_Stadium "Lia Manoliu Stadium") 2–0. Dinamo won the title in the 1999–2000 with [Adrian Mutu](/wiki/Adrian_Mutu "Adrian Mutu") playing for them but lost to [Polonia Warszawa](/wiki/Polonia_Warszawa "Polonia Warszawa") in the second qualification round of the [UEFA Champions League 1999–00](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League_1999%E2%80%9300 "UEFA Champions League 1999–00") mostly because they sold most of the players in the [Summer Mercato](/wiki/Transfer_window "Transfer window"). This affected their next season, when they lost the title to [Steaua Bucharest](/wiki/CSA_Steaua_Bucure%C8%99ti_(football) "CSA Steaua București (football)"). In 2001–02 Dinamo won the title again after a tight championship run. Dinamo managed to win the title in the last game of the season just in front of [FC National](/wiki/FC_National "FC National"). In Europe, Dinamo played in the [2001–02 UEFA Cup](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_UEFA_Cup "2001–02 UEFA Cup") eliminating [Dinamo Tirana](/wiki/Dinamo_Tirana "Dinamo Tirana") in the Qualifying round but lost in the 1st round to [Grasshopper Club Zurich](/wiki/Grasshopper_Club_Zurich "Grasshopper Club Zurich"). In the 2002–03 season Dinamo was affected again by the players who left the team, with it becoming a tradition for the leaders of the club to sell players after winning a title, losing the games in the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") and having a hard time in [Divizia A](/wiki/Divizia_A "Divizia A"). Dinamo changed a lot of managers and lost 7 consecutive games. After [Ioan Andone](/wiki/Ioan_Andone "Ioan Andone") came to the team, Dinamo played a spectacular semifinal with [Astra Ploiesti](/wiki/Astra_Ploiesti "Astra Ploiesti"). After losing 2–1 in Ploiesti in the middle of the crisis the team beat Astra in Bucharest 3–1 after extra time. They then went on to win the [Romanian Cup](/wiki/Romanian_Cup "Romanian Cup"), after beating FC National in the final 1–0 on a goal scored by [Iulian Tames](/wiki/Iulian_Tames "Iulian Tames").

After building up a team again in 2003–04, they eliminated [Shakhtar Donetsk](/wiki/FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk "FC Shakhtar Donetsk") in the first round of the [UEFA Cup 2003–04](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_2003%E2%80%9304 "UEFA Cup 2003–04") season. They went on to lose to [Spartak Moscow](/wiki/FC_Spartak_Moscow "FC Spartak Moscow") in the second round. In the Romanian League Dinamo won the title by defeating [FCSB](/wiki/FCSB "FCSB") in a close battle. They also won the Romanian Cup after a 2–0 win over [Oţelul Galaţi](/wiki/O%C5%A3elul_Gala%C5%A3i "Oţelul Galaţi") at [Cotroceni](/wiki/Cotroceni "Cotroceni").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/National_Stadium%2C_Bucharest.JPG/330px-National_Stadium%2C_Bucharest.JPG)](/wiki/File:National_Stadium,_Bucharest.JPG)

2004–05 UEFA Champions League third qualifying round match between Dinamo and [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") at Lia Manoliu stadium in Bucharest.

For the 2004–05 season Dinamo played a thrilling game vs. [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United "Manchester United") in Bucharest, at the [National Stadium](/wiki/Stadionul_Na%C8%9Bional_(1953) "Stadionul Național (1953)"), in the third qualification round of the UEFA Champions League, but lost 1–2.[[20]](#cite_note-20) This game was significant because it showed a lot of progress from the last attempts to qualify for the group phase of the Champions League. The 2nd leg was lost at [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford_(football_ground) "Old Trafford (football ground)") 3–0.

A highlight in recent times came in the [UEFA Cup 2005–06](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_2005%E2%80%9306 "UEFA Cup 2005–06") season when Dinamo thrashed [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") club [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") 5–1.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Dinamo went on to win the tie 5–2. Also, they managed to beat [CSKA Moscow](/wiki/PFC_CSKA_Moscow "PFC CSKA Moscow") (Cup Holders) 1–0 but they missed the European Spring due to a couple of close games lost in the last few seconds. The most controversial was played at [Stade Vélodrome](/wiki/Stade_V%C3%A9lodrome "Stade Vélodrome"), where Dinamo lost 2–1 against [Olympique de Marseille](/wiki/Olympique_de_Marseille "Olympique de Marseille") although [Octavian Chihaia](/wiki/Octavian_Chihaia "Octavian Chihaia") scored the equalizer in the dying seconds, but the referee didn't validate the goal because he was turn towards the center of the field, preparing to end the game.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In the 2006–07 season they did qualify for the European Spring where they were eliminated by Benfica after a 0–1 loss at [Da Luz](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_da_Luz "Estádio da Luz") and a 1–2 loss at home. Domestically, the team crushed most of its opponents in the first 19 rounds, ending up autumn champions, 13 points ahead of second place and then they secured their 18th title with four rounds to spare.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The Romanian champions could have qualified directly to the Champions League group stage for the 2007–08 season, if Manchester United or Chelsea would have won the competition in the 2006–07 season. But [AC Milan](/wiki/AC_Milan "AC Milan") became champions, and Dinamo had to play a preliminary round before the group phase.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Dinamo failed again to enter the Champions League group phase, being eliminated in the third qualifying round by [Lazio Roma](/wiki/S.S._Lazio "S.S. Lazio").[[25]](#cite_note-25) After four manager changes, Dinamo finished the 2007–08 season on the 4th spot in Liga I. In the summer of 2008, [Mircea Rednic](/wiki/Mircea_Rednic "Mircea Rednic") returned as coach having the mission to win the title and qualify Dinamo to the Champions League, but after the team finished the first part of the season as leader, they failed at the finish line and ended only third.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Rednic_Mircea.jpg/250px-Rednic_Mircea.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rednic_Mircea.jpg)

[Mircea Rednic](/wiki/Mircea_Rednic "Mircea Rednic"), the last coach that won the title with Dinamo

In the 2009–10 season, Dinamo played in the playoff for [Europa League](/wiki/Europa_League "Europa League") against Czech football club [FC Slovan Liberec](/wiki/FC_Slovan_Liberec "FC Slovan Liberec"). In the first leg the supporters invaded the pitch causing the match to be abandoned in the 88th minute when the score was 2–0 for Slovan.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The UEFA Control and Disciplinary Body awarded a default 0–3 defeat against Dinamo.[[27]](#cite_note-27) One week later in [Liberec](/wiki/Liberec "Liberec") Dinamo managed a memorable comeback and qualified in the [Europa League 2009–10](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_UEFA_Europa_League "2009–10 UEFA Europa League") group stage after winning 3–0 in Liberec after 90 and 120 minutes and winning 9–8 at penalties after 10 series.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The domestic season was yet another unsuccessful one, finishing 6th in the championships.

Dinamo finished 6th in the 2010–11 season and qualified for the [Cup](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "2010–11 Cupa României") finals against FCSB, but lost due to an own goal. For the 2011–12 season, club started with a new coach, ex-Dinamo player [Liviu Ciobotariu](/wiki/Liviu_Ciobotariu "Liviu Ciobotariu").

After a disappointing defeat against [Vorskla Poltava](/wiki/FC_Vorskla_Poltava "FC Vorskla Poltava") in the [Europa League Play-Off](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_UEFA_Europa_League "2011–12 UEFA Europa League"), Dinamo is leading the [Romanian Championship](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Liga_I "2011–12 Liga I") after 10 rounds with the best offense and defense in the championship despite selling [Gabriel Torje](/wiki/Gabriel_Torje "Gabriel Torje") to the [Italian](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") team [Udinese](/wiki/Udinese_Calcio "Udinese Calcio") with only [Dorel Stoica](/wiki/Dorel_Stoica "Dorel Stoica") and [Srdjan Luchin](/wiki/Srdjan_Luchin "Srdjan Luchin") completing the squad for the new season.

At the end of the autumn season, Dinamo was leading the table by one point ahead of [CFR Cluj](/wiki/CFR_Cluj "CFR Cluj") and started the spring one with a 2–0 win against [Gaz Metan Mediaș](/wiki/Gaz_Metan_Media%C8%99 "Gaz Metan Mediaș") in the first ever game played by the Red Dogs on the [Național Arena](/wiki/Arena_Na%C8%9Bional%C4%83 "Arena Națională") in front of a season record of 20,000 spectators that filled the first tier of the brand new stadium. Even in this circumstances, the team had a very disappointing spring run in the league and finished 5th, with 62 points in 34 matches. Still, Dinamo managed to win 2 trophies, the [Romanian Cup](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "2011–12 Cupa României"), in a final against Rapid, qualifying in the process for the [Europa League Play-off](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_UEFA_Europa_League "2012–13 UEFA Europa League") and the [Romanian Super Cup](/wiki/2012_Supercupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "2012 Supercupa României") against CFR Cluj.

### 2013–2017: Changing of ownership and insolvency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2013–2017: Changing of ownership and insolvency")]

In March 2013, businessman Ionuț Negoiță bought the club from Nicolae Badea.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The 2012–13 season ended with the same team as before the changing of the ownership, but after the season, Negoiță started his moves. He appointed former Dinamo glory [Gheorghe Mulțescu](/wiki/Gheorghe_Mul%C8%9Bescu "Gheorghe Mulțescu") as head coach, bought a new president, Constantin Anghelache and a new boss for the youth academy, Gabi Răduță. Things didn't work as hoped and in September 2013, Mulțescu was sacked. The decision came because Dinamo won only nine points in the first eight rounds of the Liga I season. Mulțescu's replacement was [Flavius Stoican](/wiki/Flavius_Stoican "Flavius Stoican") who was promoted from the second team.[[30]](#cite_note-30) With Stoican at helm, Dinamo started to climb and in March 2014, the team was close to the podium. They finished the season on the fourth spot and reached the semifinals of the Romanian Cup, where they were ousted by [FCSB](/wiki/FCSB "FCSB").

In May 2014, Ionuț Negoiță announced that the club began the procedures to enter the [insolvency](/wiki/Insolvency "Insolvency"). The Bucharest Court accepted the request in June 2014.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Thus, the Licence Committee from the Romanian Federation decided to withdraw Dinamo's rights to enter the Europa League.[[32]](#cite_note-32) On 24 September 2015, the Bucharest Municipal Court ruled that Dinamo met the criteria to end the insolvency process.[[33]](#cite_note-33) But the club wasn't able to enter the European competitions, thus, despite ending the season on the 4th position, Dinamo didn't participate in the Europa League. In the [2016–17 Liga I](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_Liga_I "2016–17 Liga I") season, Dinamo finished third in the play-off and qualified for the Europa League. In the [2017–18 UEFA Europa League](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_UEFA_Europa_League "2017–18 UEFA Europa League") season, Dinamo met the Spanish club [Athletic Bilbao](/wiki/Athletic_Bilbao "Athletic Bilbao"), in the third qualifying round. The first match, in Bucharest, ended 1–1, with the Brazilian [Rivaldinho](/wiki/Rivaldinho "Rivaldinho") scoring for Dinamo.[[34]](#cite_note-34) But Athletic won the second match 3–0 and Dinamo was eliminated.[[35]](#cite_note-35) The following two seasons in Liga I, Dinamo failed to qualify for the play-off and missed the European cups.

### 2017–present: Troubled times

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2017–present: Troubled times")]

Dinamo failed to reach the play-offs for three consecutive seasons (2018 to 2020) and with each season ended in a lower tier than before. After the ninth place in 2019, Dinamo entered the battle against relegation in the [2019–20 Liga I](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Liga_I "2019–20 Liga I") season. The owner, Ionuț Negoiță, wanted to sell the club, but nobody came with a serious proposal and the fans association bought 20% of the club's shares. [Adrian Mihalcea](/wiki/Adrian_Mihalcea "Adrian Mihalcea") was named head-coach in March 2020, but made his debut in June, after the [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") lockdown ended. Mihalcea began his tenure with four losses in the first four games in charge and Dinamo reached bottom in the championship. On 5 July, Dinamo won against Academica Clinceni and hauled themselves off the bottom position but stayed in the relegation zone. The next game ended in a draw, at home, against Politehnica Iași. Thus, Mihalcea was sacked, after only seven games in charge. [Gheorghe Mulțescu](/wiki/Gheorghe_Mul%C8%9Bescu "Gheorghe Mulțescu") came back as Dinamo's head coach, for the fourth time.

On 6 August, the Liga I season was frozen and Dinamo didn't play all its postponed games, finishing the season in 13th place. But the club was spared from relegation after the Romanian Federation decided to increase the number of teams in Liga I from 14 to 16. Thus, only the 14th place played a relegation/promotion play-off against the third place in Liga II.

On 13 August 2020, the club was purchased by Benel International SA, a Spanish company represented by Pablo Cortacero.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Dinamo transferred several contract-free players with impressive resumes and big salaries. [Cosmin Contra](/wiki/Cosmin_Contra "Cosmin Contra") came as head coach and footballers like [Borja Valle](/wiki/Borja_Valle "Borja Valle"), [Aleix Garcia](/wiki/Aleix_Garcia "Aleix Garcia"), and [Juan Camara](/wiki/Juan_Camara "Juan Camara") arrived with salaries of up to 33,000 euros per month. But Dinamo had a poor start of the season and the new owner, Pablo Cortacero, did not transfer any money to the players for several months. Almost all the foreign players submitted their memoirs so that they could leave free. Dinamo was left with huge debts, of approximately 7 million euros, and at the beginning of the 2021–22 season, the club went into insolvency again.[[37]](#cite_note-37) The [2021–22 season](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Liga_I "2021–22 Liga I") ended with a relegation to [Liga II](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II"). Dinamo ended 14th the regular season and the play-out, then played the promotion/relegation play-off against [Universitatea Cluj](/wiki/FC_Universitatea_Cluj "FC Universitatea Cluj"), third place in Liga II. U.Cluj won the first match, 2–0 at [Cluj Arena](/wiki/Cluj_Arena "Cluj Arena"). The second leg, at [Dinamo stadium](/wiki/Stadionul_Dinamo "Stadionul Dinamo"), ended 1–1. Thus, Dinamo Bucharest relegated for the first time in its history.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

In March 2022, the businessman Dorin Șerdean became the majority shareholder of Dinamo after an agreement to take over the club from Pablo Cortacero.[[39]](#cite_note-39) In February 2023, Şerdean accepted the offer received from the Red&White company to sell the club.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The owners of Red&White are Andrei Nicolescu, who owns 60% of the company, and Eugen Voicu, who owns 40%. The administrator of Red&White is Eugen Voicu.[[41]](#cite_note-41) During the promotion play-off, Dinamo defeated [Argeș Pitești](/wiki/FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti "FC Argeș Pitești") with a whopping 6–1, before they lost 4–2 in the second leg, a record breaking play-off with loads of goals, 8–5 in aggregate, which took Dinamo back to the Liga I after just one season of staying in Liga II.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

## Crest and colours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Crest and colours")]

Dinamo's colours are red and white. The current crest includes the profile of two red dogs and also a gold star above them, representing the club's tenth league title.

## Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Stadium")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Dinamo_Stadium_%282006%29.jpg/250px-Dinamo_Stadium_%282006%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dinamo_Stadium_(2006).jpg)

The Dinamo Stadium ( The Pit )

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Bucharest_Dinamo_Stadium.jpg/250px-Bucharest_Dinamo_Stadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bucharest_Dinamo_Stadium.jpg)

Dinamo's stadium in front is the statue of [Ivan Patzaichin](/wiki/Ivan_Patzaichin "Ivan Patzaichin")

Dinamo plays its home games at Stadionul Dinamo. The current Dinamo Stadium was inaugurated on 14 October 1951. The first match: Dinamo-[Locomotiva Timişoara](/wiki/CSF_CFR_Timi%C8%99oara "CSF CFR Timișoara") 1–0.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The stadium capacity was planned at 16,000, but the installation of seats decreased it to 15,032. The stadium is part of a larger complex which contains a smaller stadium, [Stadionul Florea Dumitrache](/wiki/Stadionul_Florea_Dumitrache "Stadionul Florea Dumitrache"), where the second team, Dinamo II, would play its matches. It is currently used by [CS Dinamo București rugby team](/wiki/CS_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti_(rugby) "CS Dinamo București (rugby)"). There is also a sports hall and a swimming pool.

The stadium is referred to as "Groapa" (The Pit), as it was dug rather than raising stands. The North stand is named Peluza [Cătălin Hîldan](/wiki/C%C4%83t%C4%83lin_H%C3%AEldan "Cătălin Hîldan"), in the honor of a former Dinamo player who died in 2000 at the age of 24.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Dinamo plays significant matches, such as against rivals [FCSB](/wiki/FCSB "FCSB"), at [Arena Națională](/wiki/Arena_Na%C8%9Bional%C4%83 "Arena Națională"). From 2022 onward, the team plays its home matches at the newly built [Stadionul Arcul de Triumf](/wiki/Stadionul_Arcul_de_Triumf "Stadionul Arcul de Triumf"), as its traditional ground does not meet the requirements for first league games.

## Support

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Support")]

Dinamo has an estimated 13% support in Romania, making them the second most supported Romanian club, after FCSB.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The largest concentration of fans is in Bucharest, mainly in the northeast and central areas of the city. The club also has important fan bases on other parts of the country and where significant bases of Romanians are found.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/3/38/C%C4%83t%C4%83lin_H%C3%AEldan_-_FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti.jpg/330px-C%C4%83t%C4%83lin_H%C3%AEldan_-_FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti.jpg)](/wiki/File:C%C4%83t%C4%83lin_H%C3%AEldan_-_FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti.jpg)

Dinamo fans paying homage to [Cătălin Hîldan](/wiki/C%C4%83t%C4%83lin_H%C3%AEldan "Cătălin Hîldan") in 2005.

The roots of the Dinamo ultras (fans) movement can be found in 1995 when groups like Dracula and Rams Pantelimon appeared in the North End.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 1996 a group called Nuova Guardia was formed, and became the leading group in the stadium and later on in the entire Romanian ultras movement.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Following the death of the former captain, [Cătălin Hîldan](/wiki/C%C4%83t%C4%83lin_H%C3%AEldan "Cătălin Hîldan"), in 2000, the fans renamed the North End of Dinamo's stadium to Peluza Catalin Hîldan (PCH Stand) in his honor. The majority of supporters are located in the PCH, but several factions have moved to the South End.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Rivalries & Friendships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Rivalries & Friendships")]

Main article: [Eternal derby](/wiki/Eternal_derby_(Romania) "Eternal derby (Romania)")

Dinamo's significant rivalry is with FCSB, with matches between them dubbed the [Eternal derby](/wiki/Eternal_derby_(Romania) "Eternal derby (Romania)"). Both clubs have had the most popularity in Romania with as strong reactions from its respective fans in clashes in the stadium during games and elsewhere. In 1997, Dinamo's fans set a sector of the [Stadionul Ghencea](/wiki/Stadionul_Steaua_(1974) "Stadionul Steaua (1974)") on fire.[[46]](#cite_note-46) On 16 August 2016, during FCSB's [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") 0–5 play-off loss against [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C."), Dinamo fans unveiled a banner that read *Doar Dinamo București* ("Only Dinamo Bucharest").[[47]](#cite_note-47)

The club's second most important rivalry is against [Rapid București](/wiki/FC_Rapid_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Rapid București"). In the 1990s, there was fierce competition between the two in winning the championship title.

Another rivalry is held against [Universitatea Craiova](/wiki/CS_Universitatea_Craiova "CS Universitatea Craiova"); both finished with the same number of points in the [1972–73 Divizia A](/wiki/1972%E2%80%9373_Divizia_A "1972–73 Divizia A"), but Dinamo was given the title because of their superior goal difference. A conflict has existed ever since.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) Other smaller rivalries exist, such as the one against [Petrolul Ploiești](/wiki/Petrolul_Ploie%C8%99ti "Petrolul Ploiești").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Dinamo's fans have a good friendship with [Universitatea Cluj](/wiki/Universitatea_Cluj "Universitatea Cluj")'s fans. The friendship started in the mid-1990s, both ultras groups being linked with "the mentality, fanaticism and [nationalist](/wiki/Nationalist "Nationalist") side"[[50]](#cite_note-50) and the common hate for FCSB. Dinamo have unofficial friendship with Serbian club [Crvena Zvezda](/wiki/FK_Crvena_zvezda "FK Crvena zvezda"). Friendship between this two clubs, are based on [Orthodox Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") same religion.

## Youth program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Youth program")]

Main article: [Dinamo II București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_II_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo II București")

Dinamo has an important infrastructure for training professionalism in the sport and developing interest in the youth area. The youth center organises a system based on nine age groups between nine and 18 years. Dinamo has around 180 juniors.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

All the groups play in the competitions organised by the Bucharest Football Association and in those created by the Romanian Federation. Youths around 16–18 years old are promoted to the second team, [Dinamo II](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti_II "FC Dinamo București II").

The youth center has its base in the [Dinamo Sports Center](/wiki/Dinamo_Sports_Center "Dinamo Sports Center"), where they have eight dressing rooms for the players, one for the coaches, one for the referees, a medical center and a store room for the equipment. Also, the center has many training grounds, among them the Piți Varga field.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

## Notable former players

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Notable former players")]

For a list of all former and current FC Dinamo București players with a Wikipedia article, see [Category:FC Dinamo București players](/wiki/Category:FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti_players "Category:FC Dinamo București players").

For a list of FC Dinamo București players by caps and goals, see [List of FC Dinamo București players](/wiki/List_of_FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti_players "List of FC Dinamo București players").

Dinamo's record appearance-maker is [Cornel Dinu](/wiki/Cornel_Dinu "Cornel Dinu"), who made 454 appearances between 1966 and 1983. [Ionel Dănciulescu](/wiki/Ionel_D%C4%83nciulescu "Ionel Dănciulescu") has made the second most appearances with 355.

## Notable former coaches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Notable former coaches")]

For a list of all former and current FC Dinamo București coaches with a Wikipedia article, see [Category:FC Dinamo București managers](/wiki/Category:FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti_managers "Category:FC Dinamo București managers").

For a chronological list of all former and current FC Dinamo București managers, see [List of FC Dinamo București managers](/wiki/List_of_FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti_managers "List of FC Dinamo București managers").

[Nicolae Dumitru](/wiki/Nicolae_Dumitru_(footballer) "Nicolae Dumitru (footballer)") is the most successful head coach in Dinamo's history, with five league titles and two Romanian Cups. [Ioan Andone](/wiki/Ioan_Andone "Ioan Andone") won five trophies for Dinamo, winning one title in [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I"), three Romanian Cups and one Supercup.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-Captain_57-0)** Since *Unicul Căpitan* (*The Only Captain*) died, no player will wear the number 11 shirt at Dinamo București, since the club decided to retire the shirt out of respect and posthumous honor for legend Cătălin Hîldan.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-Ekeng_58-0)** Patrick Ekeng died at [Floreasca Hospital](/wiki/Floreasca_Hospital "Floreasca Hospital") after he had gone into a coma in a match on 6 May 2016 against [Viitorul Constanța](/wiki/FC_Viitorul_Constan%C8%9Ba "FC Viitorul Constanța") where he joined as a substitute.
